passages.explanation: News travels fast. The apostles and believers throughout Judea hear that Gentiles have received the word of God. When Peter returns to Jerusalem, the circumcised believers criticise him — not for preaching to Gentiles, but for eating with them. The issue isn't theology in the abstract; it's table fellowship. Sharing a meal with uncircumcised men violates Jewish purity laws, and these early Jewish Christians still hold those boundaries tightly.

Peter explains everything step by step: the vision of the sheet, the Spirit's command, Cornelius' messengers, the angel's instructions, and most importantly, the Holy Spirit falling on the Gentiles just as it fell on the apostles at Pentecost. He tells them: if God gave them the same gift he gave us who believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to stand in God's way? The critics fall silent and then praise God: so then, even to Gentiles God has granted repentance that leads to life. Peter's defence is simple — he didn't make this decision, God did. He just didn't argue with the Holy Spirit.

Luke circles back to the scattering from Stephen's persecution. Those who scattered travel as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, but they preach the message only to Jews. However, some believers from Cyprus and Cyrene go to Antioch and start speaking to Greeks as well, telling them the good news about the Lord Jesus. The Lord's hand is with them, and a great number of people believe and turn to the Lord. Antioch becomes the first place where the gospel is intentionally and systematically preached to Gentiles by ordinary believers — not apostles.

The Jerusalem church hears about the Antioch developments and sends Barnabas to investigate. When Barnabas arrives and sees the evidence of God's grace, he's glad and encourages them to remain faithful. He's described as a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and faith, and a great number of people are brought to the Lord. Then Barnabas does something strategic: he goes to Tarsus to find Saul and brings him back to Antioch. For a whole year they meet with the church and teach great numbers. It's in Antioch that the disciples are first called Christians — the name sticks.

Prophets come down from Jerusalem to Antioch. One of them, Agabus, predicts through the Spirit that a severe famine will spread over the entire Roman world — which happens during the reign of Claudius. The disciples in Antioch decide to send relief to the believers in Judea, each contributing according to their ability. They send their gift to the elders by Barnabas and Saul. This is significant: a Gentile-majority church is now sending aid to Jewish believers. The flow of generosity has reversed, and it cements the bond between these communities.
